No sugar, no escape. JUST DHAMMA
📚Books 📜Essays 🎧Transcripts 📝Articles 🌿About 🔎Search
NE

310 - Confined Within This Body

· Transcripts · 2026-05-08 ·

Confined Within This Body

(Hillside Hermitage Talk no.310hh - edited transcript) YouTube Talk

Aṅguttara Nikāya 9.42 Sambādhasutta

Saṁyutta Nikāya 2.7 Pañcālacaṇḍasutta

Saṃyukta Āgama 470 Sallasutra

Saṁyutta Nikāya 36.6 Sallasutta

Pali Definitions

Ajahn Nyanamoli: When the Buddha wanted to describe the worst type of suffering, he would refer to the pains of dying or death. Not because of the physical unpleasantness; but because of the mental anguish. What’s the main characteristic of the discomfort of death or thoughts of dying? What’s the most unpleasant aspect of it? How can you describe it? Bhikkhu: Non-control. It threatens your existence, your sense of self. Nm: What can you experience in the world that would resemble that ultimate threat of death while you’re still alive? That would partake in exactly the same nature? B: Losing your memory, sickness…. Nm: Yes. What do all of those things have in common? B: Sense of imprisonment. Nm: Yes. Imprisonment. The main characteristic of imprisonment is confinement. Generally, confinement is the worst. Most phobias, if not all, have that confining aspect in common, you can’t move, you can’t breathe, you can’t do anything. Confined within your own body. Trapped with something you do not want. You can get glimpses of that throughout life on account of this or that, but usually it will be overlooked and ignored, until the end when you will be brought unwillingly face to face with that confinement through the experience of death or those of dying. Mental Resistance and Dispassion The reason why death, and the pains of dying, are so unpleasant is because of the confinement that the mind naturally resists and craves against. Not because it’s

1

2

unpleasant in and of itself. It might be uncomfortable, but it’s the mental anguish that makes it suffering. If the mind learns how not to move amidst confinement, then eventually even death will not be able to move it, because it’s exactly the same thing. You’re confined within your own senses. They belong to Mara: eyes, sights, ears, nose, everything. It’s a confinement that you don’t see because, while it’s a prison, you say, “Look at all the joys and pleasures I get here.” Yes, but because of those joys, you are confined, not the other way around. Once you start seeing that, you realize these are just baits that keep me confined in this environment. B: Every suffering you have is pretty much the fact that you are confined due to your craving: You can’t get what you want, you’re confined in being separated from what you want. You’re confined with what you don’t want. Nm: Yes. And as I was saying in the past, if a person wants to contemplate death, you don’t contemplate in these abstract terms, or by impersonally looking at dead bodies. Just find things that confine you. Sense restraint will be confining. Saying no to your desires will be very confining. Doing things you don’t want to do in terms of duties will be very confining. Do that. See whether your mind moves or not, and how much. That’s your training. Do not allow your mind to move amidst things that want to move you. That will eventually result in you finding an opening within the confinement. Not left, right, below, or above it. While fully confined, the mind is released from it. Turned away from it. Through dispassion. There is no other way. There’s no other mystical portal that you can escape through. It’s just through sheer dispassion amidst the confinement and discomfort of confinement. The ending of craving. If you have wisdom and patience, facing that confinement can be gradually built up. You would not necessarily suffer so sharply or immediately for a long time. But either way, sometimes you just have to. It’s not up to you to decide. As the Buddha said, for somebody, the path towards that opening, that freedom, is either going to be quick and unpleasant, quick and pleasant, slow and pleasant, or slow and unpleasant. You will know that only in hindsight, which way it was. If we were to bet on which way most people would be, two and a half millennia after the Buddha, probably it’s going to be slow and unpleasant for most people. Even that displeasure does not mean it’s going to be this excruciating torment by default. That is determined by how much you actively crave against it. It’s not a pool of pain that you have to drain and endure through. You could technically abandon it right here and now, if you’re able to face it for what it is and keep your mind dispassionate towards it, practicing that patient endurance that we so often speak about.

2

3

Understanding Dukkha and the Four Noble Truths Once you start looking at your experience authentically, you recognize that in regard to your own senses, you had no say in their arising. You certainly have no say in what they want, their desires, or how they fall apart. You’re just fully and perpetually subjected to it, pressured by it, confined by it. Confined by the duty of maintaining and feeding your desires. Otherwise, the mind will just become too unbearable. That’s like being in a hard labor prison camp. You have to work all the time. B: Is recognizing your confinement, the gradual seeing and understanding of

Dukkha?

Nm: You recognize the Dukkha, then you recognize the source of Dukkha, then you recognize the escape from Dukkha. B: And the source is your resistance. Nm: Yes. Resisting confinement, by default, at face value. As long as you have the view that this unpleasant experience that I’m pressured by, confined by, arose because such and such thing happened to it—that’s already a view that looks past the present cause of confinement and blames it on something else. First, you just have to stop acting out of those views. You might think, “I suffer because he said something that upset me.” But if you restrain yourself from acting out of that view and no longer fuel it. Then that view falls away, and you realize you suffer because suffering has arisen. That’s it. Yesterday he said something, another day somebody else didn’t do something. It’s always going to be seemingly the reason at face value, but fundamentally, I suffer because suffering is present. I suffer because I resist confinement. That’s it. The presence of resistance (to suffering) means suffering. The absence of resistance means not suffering amidst confinement. That’s exactly what the Suttas describe. Those who understand this do not suffer amidst the suffering. The Noble Eightfold Path as Taming the Mind The extent of felt confinement reveals the extent of Dukkha, because it shows the extent of your resistance towards it. That’s the Four Noble Truths. How do you tame your mind to remove any trace of resistance within confinement? Whether it’s the confinement of desire, the pressure to act out, or any other type of confinement, how do you train your mind? That’s the Noble Eightfold Path. You realize you cannot decide by will to tell this mind to stop resisting, kicking and screaming like a wild animal. But if you adhere to certain behaviors, avoid certain other behaviors, cultivate skillful things, avoid

3

4

unskillful things, eat suitable food instead of unsuitable food, and tame your senses, that animal will have to calm down. When that animal calms down, the right view will be fulfilled: the view of freedom amidst confinement. That’s Sotapanna’s understanding, which can then lead to Arahantship if he fully tames that mind within what he has already understood.

Aṅguttara Nikāya 9.42

Sambādhasutta: Confinement

The Deva’s Verse

On one occasion, Venerable Ānanda was dwelling at Kosambī in Ghosita’s Park. Then, Venerable Udāyī approached Venerable Ānanda and exchanged greetings with him. After some polite and amiable conversation, he sat to one side and said: “Friend, the young deva Pañcālacaṇḍa said this: ‘The one of vast wisdom has found an opening in confinement [crowdedness / restriction]; The awakened one who awakened to jhāna, The withdrawn chief sage.’ What is the confinement, and what is the finding of an opening in confinement spoken of by the Blessed One?” The Cords of Kāma “These five cords of kāma are the confinement spoken of by the Blessed One. What five? Forms cognizable by eye-viññāṇa that are desirable, lovely, pleasing, agreeable, accompanied by kāma, and enticing. Sounds cognizable by ear-viññāṇa… Odors cognizable by nose-viññāṇa… Tastes cognizable by tongue-viññāṇa…

4

5

Tangibles cognizable by kāya-viññāṇa that are desirable, lovely, pleasing, agreeable, accompanied by kāma, and enticing. These five cords of kāma are the confinement spoken of by the Blessed One.

The First Jhāna

“Here, a bhikkhu, withdrawn from kāma and withdrawn from unbeneficial dhammā, enters and remains in the first jhāna, which is accompanied by vitakka and vicara, and possesses pīti and sukha born of that viveka. To this extent, the finding of an opening in confinement was spoken of by the Blessed One provisionally [in a relative sense]. Yet there is still confinement there. And what is the confinement there? Whatever vitakka and vicara have not ceased there, that is the confinement here.

The Second Jhāna

“Furthermore, with the stilling of vitakka and vicara, a bhikkhu enters and remains in the second jhāna, which has internal confidence and collectedness of citta, is without vitakka and vicara, and has pīti and sukha born of that samādhi. To this extent, the finding of an opening in confinement was spoken of by the Blessed One provisionally. Yet there is still confinement there. And what is the confinement there? Whatever pīti has not ceased there, that is the confinement here.

The Third Jhāna

“Furthermore, with dispassion for pīti, he dwells with upekkhā, sati, clear comprehension, and experiences sukha with the kāya. He enters and remains in the third jhāna, of which the Noble Ones declare: ‘He dwells with upekkhā, sati, and in sukha.’ To this extent, the finding of an opening in confinement was spoken of by the Blessed One provisionally. Yet there is still confinement there. And what is the confinement there? Whatever upekkhā-sukha has not ceased there, that is the confinement here.

The Fourth Jhāna

“Furthermore, with the abandoning of sukha and dukkha, and with the previous disappearance of somanassa and domanassa, he enters and remains in the fourth

5

6

jhāna, which is neither dukkha nor sukha, and has purity of sati and upekkhā. To this extent, the finding of an opening in confinement was spoken of by the Blessed One provisionally. Yet there is still confinement there. And what is the confinement there? Whatever form-saññā has not ceased there, that is the confinement here.

The Formless Dimensions

“Furthermore, by completely surmounting form-saññās, with the passing away of sensory impact-saññās, and with non-attention to diversity-saññās, aware that ‘space is infinite,’ a bhikkhu enters and remains in the base of infinite space. To this extent, the finding of an opening in confinement was spoken of by the Blessed One provisionally. Yet there is still confinement there. And what is the confinement there? Whatever saññā of the base of infinite space has not ceased there, that is the confinement here. “Furthermore, by completely surmounting the base of infinite space, aware that ‘viññāṇa is infinite,’ a bhikkhu enters and remains in the base of infinite viññāṇa. To this extent, the finding of an opening in confinement was spoken of by the Blessed One provisionally. Yet there is still confinement there. And what is the confinement there? Whatever saññā of the base of infinite viññāṇa has not ceased there, that is the confinement here. “Furthermore, by completely surmounting the base of infinite viññāṇa, aware that ‘there is nothing,’ a bhikkhu enters and remains in the base of nothingness. To this extent, the finding of an opening in confinement was spoken of by the Blessed One provisionally. Yet there is still confinement there. And what is the confinement there? Whatever saññā of the base of nothingness has not ceased there, that is the confinement here. “Furthermore, by completely surmounting the base of nothingness, a bhikkhu enters and remains in the base of neither-saññā-nor-non-saññā. To this extent, the finding of an opening in confinement was spoken of by the Blessed One provisionally. Yet there is still confinement there. And what is the confinement there? Whatever saññā of the base of neither-saññā-nor-non-saññā has not ceased there, that is the confinement here.

6

7

Cessation and Liberation “Furthermore, by completely surmounting the base of neither-saññā-nor-non-saññā, a bhikkhu enters and remains in the cessation of saññā and vedanā. And having seen with wisdom, the influxes [taints] are completely destroyed. To this extent, the finding of an opening in confinement was spoken of by the Blessed One definitively [in an absolute sense].”

Saṁyutta Nikāya 2.7 Pañcālacaṇḍasutta: Pañcālacaṇḍa

Originating at Sāvatthī. Standing to one side, the devaputta Pañcālacaṇḍa recited this verse in the presence of the Blessed One:

The Devaputta’s Verse

“Amidst confinement, the one of vast wisdom has indeed found an opening. The awakened one, the sage who is a withdrawn bull, awakened to jhana.”

The Buddha’s Reply

“Pañcālacaṇḍa,” the Blessed One replied, “even amidst confinement they find the Dhamma for the attainment of nibbāna. Those who have acquired sati are rightly and fully samāhita.”

Saṃyukta Āgama 470 Sallasutra: The Arrow

The Worldling and the Noble Disciple Thus have I heard: On one occasion, the Buddha was staying in Rājagaha at the

Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the assembly: “Foolish, untaught worldlings experience dukkha vedanā, sukha vedanā, and neither-dukkha-nor-sukha vedanā. The well-taught ariyasāvako also experiences

7

8

dukkha vedanā, sukha vedanā, and neither-dukkha-nor-sukha vedanā. What, then, is the difference between the common worldling and the noble person?” They addressed the Buddha: “The World-Honored One is the root of the dhammā, the eye of the dhammā, and the refuge of the dhammā. Excellent! World-Honored One, we only wish that you would speak extensively on this. Having heard it, we will receive, uphold, and practice it.” The Double Arrow of the Worldling The Buddha told them: “When the foolish, untaught worldling’s kāya makes phassa, various vedanā arise. Oppressed by the pain of dukkha—even to the point of losing their life—they sorrow, grieve, weep, and cry out, wailing in resentment and complaint. “Listen carefully and consider it well; I will speak to you. When the foolish, untaught worldling’s kāya makes phassa and various vedanā arise, the pain of dukkha increases, even to the point of taking their life. Sorrowing, grieving, complaining, and resenting, they weep, cry, and wail, and their citta falls into madness and confusion. Right at that time, two kinds of vedanā increase: kāya vedanā and citta vedanā. “Suppose a man’s kāya is struck by two poisoned arrows, and extreme pain and dukkha arise. The foolish, untaught worldling is exactly like this. Two vedanā—kāya vedanā and citta vedanā—increase, and extreme pain and dukkha arise. “Why is this so? Because that foolish, untaught worldling does not truly understand. When making phassa with the five objects of kāma, a sukha vedanā arises. Because they experience the sukha of the five kāma, they are driven by the underlying tendency of rāga. Because they make phassa with a dukkha vedanā, dosa arises. Because dosa arises, they are driven by the underlying tendency of dosa. “Regarding these two vedanā, they do not truly know their origination, cessation, gratification, danger, and escape as they really are. Because they do not know them as they really are, neither-dukkha-nor-sukha vedanā arises, and they are driven by the underlying tendency of moha.

8

9

“Bound by sukha vedanā, they ultimately do not escape it. Bound by dukkha vedanā, they ultimately do not escape it. Bound by neither-dukkha-nor-sukha vedanā, they ultimately do not escape it. By what are they bound? They are bound by rāga, dosa, and moha. They are bound by birth, aging, illness, death, sorrow, lamentation, despair, and dukkha. The Single Arrow of the Noble Disciple “When the well-taught ariyasāvako’s kāya makes phassa, a dukkha vedanā arises. Though oppressed by great dukkha—even to the point of taking their life—they do not give rise to sorrow, lamentation, complaints, or resentment. They do not weep, cry, or wail, nor does their citta become confused or go mad. Right at that time, only one vedanā arises: namely, kāya vedanā. Citta vedanā does not arise. “Suppose a man is struck by a single poisoned arrow but is not struck by a second poisoned arrow. Right at that time, only one vedanā arises: namely, kāya vedanā. Citta vedanā does not arise. “When making phassa with a sukha vedanā, they are not defiled by the sukha of kāma. Because they are not defiled by the sukha of kāma, the underlying tendency of rāga toward that sukha vedanā does not drive them. When experiencing dukkha phassa vedanā, dosa does not arise. Because dosa does not arise, the underlying tendency of dosa does not drive them. “Regarding those two underlying tendencies, they know the origin, cessation, gratification, danger, and escape as they really are. Because they know them as they really are, the underlying tendency of moha toward neither-dukkha-nor-sukha vedanā does not drive them. Regarding that sukha vedanā, it is liberated and unbound. Regarding the dukkha vedanā and the neither-dukkha-nor-sukha vedanā, it is liberated and unbound. “From what is it unbound? It is unbound from rāga, dosa, and moha. It is unbound from birth, aging, illness, death, sorrow, lamentation, despair, and dukkha.” Verses of Summary At that time, the World-Honored One then spoke in verse:

9

10

“The much-hearing one, toward dukkha and sukha, Does not lack vedanā or saññā; But compared to the ordinary worldlings, They truly possess great learning. In sukha vedanā, they are not heedless, And dukkha phassa does not increase their sorrow. Toward both dukkha and sukha, they maintain upekkhā, Neither complying with nor opposing them. The bhikkhu diligently employs skillful means, With right knowledge that neither inclines nor shakes. Toward all of these vedanā, Their sharp wisdom is able to clearly comprehend. Because they comprehend the various vedanā, In the present dhammā, they exhaust the various taints [āsavas]. When the kāya dies, they do not fall into any reckoning [category], Having reached the finality [irreversible state] of pari-nibbāna.” After the Buddha had spoken this sutra, the bhikkhus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and undertook to practice it.

Saṁyutta Nikāya 36.6 Sallasutta: The Arrow

The Two Kinds of Experience “The uninstructed worldling vedayati a sukha vedanā, vedayati a dukkha vedanā, and vedayati an adukkhamasukha vedanā. The instructed ariyasāvako also vedayati a sukha vedanā, vedayati a dukkha vedanā, and vedayati an adukkhamasukha vedanā. What, then, is the distinction, the disparity, and the difference between the instructed ariyasāvako and the uninstructed worldling?” “Venerable sir, our dhammā are rooted in the Blessed One…”

10

11

The Worldling and the Second Arrow “When the uninstructed worldling is touched by a dukkha vedanā, they sorrow, languish, lament, cry beating their breast, and undergo bewilderment. They vedayati two vedanās: kāyika and cetasika. It is just as if they were to pierce a man with an arrow [dart], and then they were to pierce him with a second arrow following it. Thus, that man would vedayati the vedanā of two arrows. In exactly the same way, when the uninstructed worldling is touched by a dukkha vedanā, they sorrow, languish, lament, cry beating their breast, and undergo bewilderment. They vedayati two vedanās: kāyika and cetasika. When they are touched by that very dukkha vedanā, they have aversion/resistance toward it. For one who has aversion toward a dukkha vedanā, whatever underlying tendency to aversion there is toward dukkha vedanā underlies them. When they are touched by a dukkha vedanā, they delight in kāmasukha. Why is that? Because the uninstructed worldling does not understand any escape [release] from dukkha vedanā other than kāmasukha. As they delight in kāmasukha, whatever rāgānusayo there is toward sukha vedanā underlies them. They do not understand the origin [arising], the passing away [subsiding], the gratification [enjoyment], the danger [drawback], and the escape from these vedanās as it actually is. As they do not understand this, whatever avijjānusayo there is toward adukkhamasukha vedanā underlies them. If they vedayati a sukha vedanā, they vedayati tied to it. If they vedayati a dukkha vedanā, they vedayati tied to it. If they vedayati an adukkhamasukha vedanā, they vedayati tied to it. This is called an uninstructed worldling who is tied to birth, aging, and death, to sorrows, laments, dukkhas, domanassas, and despairs; they are tied to dukkha, I say. The Noble Disciple and the Single Arrow When the instructed ariyasāvako is touched by a dukkha vedanā, they do not sorrow, languish, lament, cry beating their breast, or undergo bewilderment. They vedayati one vedanā: kāyika, not cetasika.

11

12

It is just as if they were to pierce a man with an arrow, but they would not pierce him with a second arrow following it. Thus, that man would vedayati the vedanā of one arrow. In exactly the same way, when the instructed ariyasāvako is touched by a dukkha vedanā, they do not sorrow, languish, lament, cry beating their breast, or undergo bewilderment. They vedayati one vedanā: kāyika, not cetasika. When they are touched by that very dukkha vedanā, they do not have aversion toward it. Because they have no aversion toward dukkha vedanā, the underlying tendency to aversion toward dukkha vedanā does not underlie them. When they are touched by a dukkha vedanā, they do not delight in kāmasukha. Why is that? Because the instructed ariyasāvako understands the escape from dukkha vedanā apart from kāmasukha. As they do not delight in kāmasukha, the rāgānusayo toward sukha vedanā does not underlie them. They understand the origin, the passing away, the gratification, the danger, and the escape from these vedanās as it actually is. Because they understand this, the avijjānusayo toward adukkhamasukha vedanā does not underlie them. If they vedayati a sukha vedanā, they vedayati untied from it. If they vedayati a dukkha vedanā, they vedayati untied from it. If they vedayati an adukkhamasukha vedanā, they vedayati untied from it. This is called an instructed ariyasāvako who is untied from birth, aging, and death, from sorrows, laments, dukkhas, domanassas, and despairs; they are untied from dukkha, I say. This is the distinction, the disparity, and the difference between the instructed ariyasāvako and the uninstructed worldling.” Verses of the Wise “The wise one, highly learned, Does not vedayati the vedanā—whether sukha or dukkha; This is the great distinction in skillfulness [wholesomeness] Between the wise one and the worldling [ordinary person]. For the highly learned one who is saṅkhātadhamma (taken the measure of things), Who is vipassato (sees through) into this world and the next;

12

13

Agreeable dhammā do not churn their citta, And they do not go to aversion toward the disagreeable. For them, favouring [liking] and oppositions [disliking] Are smoked out, brought to an end, and do not exist; Having known the stainless, sorrowless state, The crosser-over of bhava understands rightly.”

Pali Definitions:

1. Adukkhamasukha

● Etymology: a- (not) + dukkha (pain/unease) + ma (sandhi/linking letter) + sukha (pleasure/ease).

● Literal Meaning: Not-painful-not-pleasant.

2. Arahant / Arahantship

● Etymology: From the root arah- (to be worthy) or a compound of ari (enemy) + hant (killer). ● Literal Meaning: The worthy one; or the destroyer of enemies

(defilements).

3. Ariyasāvako

● Etymology: ariya (noble) + sāvaka (hearer/listener, from the root śru, to hear). ● Literal Meaning: Noble listener; Noble disciple.

  • Āsava (Āsavas) ● Etymology: ā- (towards/into) + the root su (to flow or press out). ● Literal Meaning: Influx, outflow, fermentation, or leak.

    5. Avijjānusayo

    ● Etymology: a- (not) + vijjā (knowledge) + anusaya (from anu- [along] + root si [to lie down]). ● Literal Meaning: The underlying tendency of non-knowledge or ignoring (ignore: in-(not), gnar-(knowing))

    13

14

6. Bhava

● Etymology: From the root bhū (to be, to become). ● Literal Meaning: Existence, being.

7. Bhikkhu

● Etymology: From the root bhikkh- (to beg or ask for a share). ● Literal Meaning: Mendicant; one who relies on shared food.

8. Cetasika

● Etymology: Derived from citta + the adjectival suffix -ika. ● Literal Meaning: Pertaining to the mind.

9. Citta

● Etymology: From the root cit (to think, perceive, or be aware).

● Literal Meaning: Mind.

10. Deva / Devaputta

● Etymology: From the root div (to shine). Putta means son or child. ● Literal Meaning: Shining one; Son of a shining one.

11. Saṅkhātadhamma

● Etymology: sam- (together) + the root khā (to measure, count, reckon) + dhamma (nature/phenomena).

● Literal Meaning: One who has taken the measure of phenomena.

12. Domanassa

● Etymology: du- (bad/difficult) + manas (intellect,mental). ● Literal Meaning: Bad-mindedness; mental pain or displeasure.

13. Dosa

● Etymology: From the root duṣ (to spoil, corrupt, or be bad). ● Literal Meaning: Spoiling, corruption, aversion, anger.

14. Dukkha

● Etymology: du- (bad/difficult) + kha (hole, space, or axle-hole).

14

15

● Literal Meaning: Hard to bear; a bad axle-hole (implying friction and a bumpy ride); unease.

15. Jhāna

● Etymology: From the root dhyā or jhā (to burn, to contemplate, to comprehend). ● Literal Meaning: Burning (up of defilements); composed comprehension.

16. Kāma / Kāmasukha

● Etymology: From the root kam (to desire, wish, or love). ● Literal Meaning: Sensuality, desire, or longing. Kāmasukha = the pleasure of sensuality.

17. Kāya / Kāyika

● Etymology: From the root ci (to heap, pile up, or accumulate). ● Literal Meaning: A heap, a collection, a body. Kāyika = bodily / pertaining to the physical collection.

18. Māra

● Etymology: From the root mṛ (to die). ● Literal Meaning: The killer, death, the destroyer.

19. Moha

● Etymology: From the root muh (to be confused, stupified, or deluded). ● Literal Meaning: Delusion, stupor, confusion, bewilderment.

20. Nibbāna / Pari-nibbāna

● Etymology: nir- (out) + root vā (to blow). ● Literal Meaning: Blowing out, extinguishing, cooling down, unbinding. Pari- implies finality.

21. Pīti

● Etymology: From the root prī (to please or satisfy). ● Literal Meaning: Pleasing, joy, satisfaction.

22. Phassa

● Etymology: From the root spṛś (to touch). ● Literal Meaning: Touch, contact, pressure.

15

16

23. Rāga / Rāgānusayo

● Etymology: From the root raj (to color, dye, or glow). Anusaya means underlying tendency. ● Literal Meaning: Dye/color, passion, lust. Rāgānusayo = the underlying tendency to passion.

24. Samādhi / Samāhita

● Etymology: sam- (together) + ā- (towards) + root dhā (to place or put). ● Literal Meaning: Placing together, collectedness, composure. Samāhita = collected, composed.

25. Saññā

● Etymology: sam- (together) + the root jñā (to know). ● Literal Meaning: Knowing together, perception, recognition, or sign.

26. Sati

● Etymology: From the root smṛ (to remember or recollect). ● Literal Meaning: Memory, recollection, bearing in mind.

27. Somanassa

● Etymology: su- (good/easy) + manas (intellect, mental). ● Literal Meaning: Good-mindedness; mental pleasure or joy.

28. Sotāpanna

● Etymology: 1- Sota (stream): from the root śru (to flow); 2- Sota (Ear): From the root śru (to hear)

  • āpanna (entered or attained). ● Literal Meaning: One who has entered the stream; or one who has “attained the hearing” of the Dhamma.

29. Sukha

● Etymology: su- (good/easy) + kha (hole, space, or axle-hole). ● Literal Meaning: Easy, pleasant; a good axle-hole (a smooth ride).

30. Upekkhā

● Etymology: upa- (over, near, or upon) + the root īkṣ (to look or see). ● Literal Meaning: Looking over, overseeing, equanimity.

31. Vedanā / Vedayati

16

17

● Etymology: From the root vid (to know, to feel, or to experience). ● Literal Meaning: Feeling, experiencing. Vedayati is the active verb: “to feel” or “to experience.”

32. Vicāra

● Etymology: vi- (prefix denoting separation or variety) + the root car (to move or wander). ● Literal Meaning: Wandering, moving around, exploring, evaluating, contemplating.

33. Viññāṇa

● Etymology: vi- (apart/divided) + ñāṇa (knowing). ● Literal Meaning: Divided knowing, discriminative consciousness, or cognizance.

34. Vipassato

● Etymology: vi- (apart, clearly) + the root dṛś / passati (to see). This is the present participle form. ● Literal Meaning: Seeing clearly; seeing distinctly, seeing through.

35. Vitakka

● Etymology: vi- (prefix) + the root tark (to reason, turn, or think). ● Literal Meaning: thought, thinking, or directing the mind.

36. Viveka

● Etymology: vi- (apart/away) + the root vic (to separate). ● Literal Meaning: Separation, detachment, seclusion, withdrawal.

Just Dhamma

A quiet place for study, reflection, and practice.
